text.title: Architecture Decision Process Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the decision-tree-process methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Before any option is listed, the decision MUST be stated as a single answerable question, MUST list out-of-scope items, and MUST be classified as one-way or two-way door.

rationale.source: Bezos one-way / two-way door framing; ADR practice.

rule.rationale: Vague problem statements produce vague options; a single question forces clarity and makes the decision auditable later.

rule.statement: Options generation MUST include 'do nothing' with its documented cost, MUST include at least one unconventional option, and MUST shortlist 2-5 viable options.
rule.rationale: Without 'do nothing', teams systematically overweight the value of change; without an unconventional option, breakthrough solutions are never considered.

rule.statement: The weighted decision matrix MUST have weights that sum to 100%, weights MUST be set by stakeholders (not the architect alone), every score MUST cite evidence, and at least one cross-criteria conflict MUST be named.
rule.rationale: Un-weighted matrices implicitly weight everything equally; stakeholder weighting moves the value call to its rightful owner; cited evidence prevents post-hoc rationalisation.

rule.statement: High-impact one-way-door decisions MUST include a time-boxed POC with explicit success criteria before the ADR moves to Accepted. POC scope MUST be minimal: only integration risks + perf-critical paths.

rationale.source: Internal POC postmortems; Lean Startup MVP framing.

rule.rationale: Analysis alone cannot surface unknown unknowns; POCs catch integration incompatibilities and team capability gaps the matrix cannot model.

rule.statement: Every significant decision MUST land as an ADR in version control with status, context, options, decision, consequences, and a scheduled review date (6 / 12 / 24 months by reversibility).
rule.rationale: Without version-controlled ADRs, teams relitigate the same decision and lose the reasoning chain when members leave.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (single-question problem, stakeholder list, constraints, reversibility), run the six phases in order. Skipping a phase is a process error.
rule.rationale: Each phase closes one specific failure mode (scope creep, premature commitment, hidden trade-off, stakeholder surprise, institutional amnesia, drift).

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) decision is trivial / reversible, (b) the decision is already made and working, (c) emergency time-pressure. Note the constraint and exit; document retroactively if needed.
rule.rationale: The six-phase process is overhead that pays back only on high-impact or hard-to-reverse decisions.
